result:
The morning light filters through the leaves like spilled honey, and I’m thinking about how strange it is that we’re all just walking around carrying entire universes inside our skulls. Yesterday I watched a crow solve a three-step puzzle for a piece of cheese and felt genuinely humbled by its cleverness. Today I’m eating cold pizza for breakfast and pretending it’s a sophisticated choice.

I keep collecting small wonders the way other people collect stamps: the particular way my cat sighs when she gives up on being dignified, the smell of rain on hot asphalt, how perfectly a good song can crack you open like an egg. There’s this quiet rebellion in noticing things. In a world optimized for distraction, choosing to actually pay attention feels almost radical.

Sometimes I lie on the floor of my apartment and stare at the ceiling cracks until they start looking like rivers on a map. I imagine sailing down them in a tiny boat, waving at the dust-bunny villagers. It’s ridiculous. It’s perfect. The older I get, the more I suspect that maintaining some relationship with your own absurdity might be one of the healthiest things a person can do.

The world is so loud. But every once in a while, if you’re very still, you can hear something else underneath it—like the planet itself is humming an old, patient song. I don’t know what it’s singing about. Maybe it’s just glad we’re here. Maybe it’s been waiting for us to notice.

(Word count: 248)
